1 Defendants’ Motion for Summary Judgment, or in the alternative, for Summary
2 Adjudication of Issues came on for hearing on February 17, 2005, at 9:30 a.m., in Department
; 302 of the above-captioned court, the Honorable Ronald E. Quidachay presiding. Kelly H.
Armstrong of The Armstrong Law Firm and Zachary Best of the Law Offices of Waukeen Q.
6 || McCoy appeared on behalf of Plaintiff Patricia Westerfield. Michael Vartain of Vartain Law
7 || Group appeared on behalf of defendants. Following the arguments of counsel, the court took the
8 || matter under submission. .
. After reviewing the briefs, supporting papers and evidence submitted by Plaintiff Patricia
11 Westerfield and defendants, and considering the argument of counsel at the hearing on this
12 || motion, the court hereby finds that triable issues of material fact exist in this case and that
13 || defendants’ motion is denied on the following grounds:
\4 1. There are triable issues of material fact regarding Plaintiff's claim for age
. discrimination. To prevail on a claim for age discrimination a plaintiff must establish a prima
17 facie case of discrimination by showing an intentional act by an employer in which the motive of
18 || the employer was to take action against the employee because of her age. Mixon v. Fair
19 Employment and Housing Commission (1987) 192 Cal.App.3d 1306, 1317. Plaintiff has shifted
20 the burden to the defense. Here, defendants, made numerous derogatory age related comments
. to Westerfield by telling her she must be in menopause, older people just do not get it, another
93 || older person was like an old kindergarten teacher, older people like to live in the suburbs,
24 || another older woman was an old battle axe, she should come to a party and dance with an older .
25 employee who dressed like they did in the 1970’s and at her age she would just as soon not
. celebrate her birthday. There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether multiple
28 comments led to the elimination of Plaintiffs position by defendants because defendant Nicole
2

 
. ~~ 2
1 || Bohn had a bias against older employees. Defendants claim that Plaintiffs position was
2 || eliminated due to increased student needs. However, there is a triable issue as to whether
; defendants’ stated reason is a pretext for discrimination because Plaintiff has rebutted their
alleged nondiscriminatory basis for terminating her position. Defendants eliminated Plaintiff's
6 || position in June 2001, seven months before they offered the February 2002 student needs
7 || assessment as the basis for her termination. Plaintiff has also submitted evidence that defendants
8 || falsified the student numbers to justify their conclusion of increased student needs and
- defendants admit in their own needs assessment report that the report was based upon
ll speculation and conjecture. Here, Plaintiff has produced sufficient evidence to create triable
12 ||issues of material fact. Finally, there is a triable issue of fact as to whether Defendants hired an
13 || individual in Plaintiff's protected class only after Plaintiff complaint of potential age
M4 discrimination.
15
6 2. There are triable issues of material fact regarding Plaintiff's claim for wrongful
17 termination in violation of public policy because Plaintiff has: (1) established a prima facie case
18 || for age discrimination and (2) established a prima facie case for retaliation as discussed below.
19 || Plaintiff does not need to establish that she was discriminated against based on her age to prevail
70 on her retaliation claim. Therefore, Plaintiff may proceed with a wrongful termination in
. violation of public policy claim based on her retaliation claim alone.
3 3. There are triable issues of material fact regarding Plaintiff's claim for retaliation
24 || due to the proximity in time between when Plaintiff complained of discrimination and
25 harassment in April 2001 and defendants’ decision to terminate her from her employment just
. two months later in June 2001. The proximity in time between Plaintiff's complaints to
28 defendant Dena Davis regarding defendant Nicole Bohn’s conduct and the elimination of her
3

 
1 || position by defendant B.J. Johnson creates an inference of retaliation. Jordan v. Clark

2 (9""Cir.1988) 847 F.2d 1368, 1376. There is a nexus between Plaintiff's protected activity and

; the adverse employment action taken against her by defendants. Defendants have never offered |

a defense or explanation as to why they eliminated Plaintiffs position just two months after she

6 || complained, which further allows Plaintiff's claim for retaliation to proceed. Plaintiff is not

7 || required to prove that she was discriminated against based on her age in order to prevail on a

8 claim for retaliation, but that she was retaliated against for engaging in the protected activity of
. complaining of discrimination and harassment under FEHA. Further, Defendants claims to have
11 offered Plaintiff a position which Defendants then removed without explanation.
12 4. There are triable issues of material fact regarding Plaintiffs claim for harassment.
13 || Plaintiff has shifted the burden to defendants by submitting evidence that defendant Bohn
M4 engaged in a pattern of conduct which was sufficiently severe and pervasive to create triable
. issues of material fact as to the severity of Bohn’s treatment of Plaintiff. Bohn routinely made
17 derogatory age-related comments about Plaintiff, or in Plaintiff's presence, as discussed above.
18 || Plaintiff has also submitted evidence that Bohn screamed at Plaintiff and humiliated her on a
19 daily basis until she was terminated. Plaintiff was forced to tie Bohn’s shoe laces and perform
20 other humiliating and embarrassing tasks in contrast to the other employees in the department.
. Bohn’s mistreatment of Plaintiff was also witnessed by three student workers as submitted in
93 || Plaintiff's evidence. Plaintiff's evidence regarding defendants derogatory age related comments
24 || and Bohn’s mistreatment of her creates triable issues as to whether defendants created a hostile
25 |! work environment based on age. The continuing violations doctrine brings Plaintiff within the
. statute of limitations because Plaintiff has submitted evidence that defendants harassed her
28

4

 
-, i

1 || through the date her employment terminated. Morgan v. Regents of University of California

2 |! (2000) 88 C.A. 4" 52, 64.

; 5. There are triable issues of material fact regarding Plaintiff's claim for breach of

contract, which are not predicated upon Plaintiff's claim for age discrimination because it is a

6 || Separate claim. Plaintiff had an employment agreement whereby her position could not be

7 || eliminated unless the disabled student needs changed as admitted by defendants. Defendants

8 rely upon a February 2002 disabled student needs assessment report, which states that the studen
. numbers and services needed increased. However, Plaintiff reviewed the documents relied upon
Hl by defendants for their report and has submitted evidence that the increased student numbers are
12 || false. There is a triable issue of material fact as to whether the student needs changed in a
13 || manner which justified the elimination of Plaintiff's position. Additionally, defendants
14 eliminated Plaintiff's position in June 2001 — seven months before the needs assessment report
. was issued. Therefore, there is also a triable issue of material fact as to whether Plaintiffs
17 position was eliminated due to student needs. Defendants admit in the February 2002 needs
18 || assessment report that the student numbers are based upon speculation and conjecture, which
19 || creates another triable issue of material fact. Plaintiff did not fail to exhaust her administrative
20 remedies because she requested an appeal of the administrative process, which defendants failed
. to perform.
23 6. There are triable issues of material fact regarding Plaintiff's claim for breach of
24 || the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing for the same reasons as those which support
25 || the breach of contract claim.
i. 7. There are triable issues of material fact regarding Plaintiffs claim for intentional
28 infliction of emotional distress as to whether the treatment she was subjected to by defendants

5

 
1 || was sufficiently extreme and outrageous to meet the legal standard. Plaintiffs intentional

2 || infliction of emotional distress claim is not predicated upon the establishment of her FEHA

; claims. There are triable issues of material fact concerning whether Plaintiff’s claim for

intentional infliction of emotional distress meets the following elements: (1) extreme and

6 || Outrageous conduct by the defendant with the intention of causing, or reckless disregard of the

7 || probability of causing, emotional distress; (2) the plaintiff's suffering severe or extreme

8 emotional distress and (3) actual and proximate causation of the emotional distress by the
- defendant’s conduct. Christensen v. Superior Court 54 Cal.3d 868, 903 (1991).
11 8. There are triable issues of material fact regarding whether defendants are liable
12 || for failure to investigate pursuant to Cal. Government Code § 12940(k) because defendants
13 || admittedly failed to investigate Plaintiff's April 2001 complaints of discrimination and
4 harassment.
15
16 9. Defendants’ motion for summary adjudication as to the 26the affirmative defense
17 of after acquired evidence is granted. Defendants have established through admissible evidence
18 || that Plaintiff engaged in misconduct during her employment that, if then known, would have
19 || caused Plaintiff's lawful discharge from employment. However, this affirmative defense does
20 not dispose of Plaintiffs causes of action. This defense may limit Plaintiffs amount and nature
. of damages, based on when Defendants discovered Plaintiff's misconduct.
33 10. In making this decision, the Court has specifically considered the parties’ evidence
24 || and the objection to that evidence. The Court’s decision has been based only on admissible,
25 relevant evidence. Biljac Associates v. First Interstate Bank (1990) 218 Cal. App. 3d 1410.
. For all of the foregoing reasons, disputed issues of material fact exist in this case and
28 defendants’ motion for summary judgment is denied.

6

 
. : ed a
1 Plaintiff is to prepare an order that complies with CCP section 437c(g). Further Plaintiff
2 IHis to prepare an order on her demurrer heard on February 17, 2005.
; IT IS HEREBY ORDERED that defendants’ motion for summary judgment, or, in the
alternative, for summary adjudication of issues is DENIED.
7 || Dated: —;
8 Hon. Ronald E. Quidachay
Judge of the Superior Court
